spamInfo.whyDesc: Traditional CAPTCHA services like reCAPTCHA send user data to external servers — usually Google. This raises privacy concerns, creates a third-party dependency, and can be an accessibility barrier for some users. Instead, all spam protection here runs server-side with no external services involved.
spamInfo.layer1Desc: Automated agents that blindly fill in form fields are identified — without any visible challenge for the user.
spamInfo.layer2Desc: Submissions that arrive unnaturally fast after the page loads are rejected server-side.
spamInfo.layer3Desc: A simple arithmetic question is delivered alongside an HMAC-signed token. The server validates both the answer and the token's integrity — without sessions or cookies.
spamInfo.layer4Desc: The number of submissions per IP address is capped within a time window to prevent bulk attempts.
spamInfo.hmacDesc: The token accompanying the math challenge is signed server-side using a secret key (HMAC-SHA256). Without that key, no valid token can be produced — it cannot be forged on the client. Validation happens exclusively on the server.

verifier.metaDescription: Verify ML-DSA-65 post-quantum signatures from this blog — for articles and the CBOM alike.
verifier.label: // signature verification
verifier.description: All articles on this blog are signed with ML-DSA-65 (NIST FIPS 204). You can independently verify any signature here — using only the public key and the article content, without trusting this website.
verifier.intro: Cryptographic signatures create trust without a leap of faith. Each signature binds an article to its original state — regardless of whether the CMS, server, or database is ever compromised. Since ML-DSA-65 is post-quantum secure, this guarantee remains valid even as powerful quantum computers become a reality. Verification runs entirely in your browser without contacting external servers.
verifier.descriptionCbom: The Cryptography Bill of Materials (CBOM) is also signed with ML-DSA-65 (NIST FIPS 204) after every automated update. You can independently verify that the CBOM you are viewing matches the one generated by the scanner — without trusting this website.
verifier.introCbom: The scanner writes cbom.json and immediately signs its exact UTF-8 content with the same private key used for articles. The signature is stored in cbom.sig alongside the CBOM. Verification fetches both files directly from this server and runs ML-DSA-65 entirely in your browser — no external servers, no trust required.
verifier.messageFormatDesc: These four fields are concatenated with | and passed as raw UTF-8 bytes directly to ML-DSA-65 — no pre-hashing. ML-DSA processes the message internally with SHAKE-256 (FIPS 204 §5.2). Media files are embedded as SHA-256 hashes within the blocks. Title and block content are fetched automatically from Strapi — you only need to enter the document ID.
verifier.messageFormatDescCbom: The signed message is the exact UTF-8 byte sequence of cbom.json as written by the scanner (JSON.stringify with 2-space indentation). Nothing is added or removed — what you verify is exactly what is served at /cbom.json.
verifier.resultValid: Signature valid — content matches the signature.
verifier.resultInvalid: Signature invalid — content may have been modified.
verifier.resultError: Verification failed — public key missing or input invalid.
verifier.cbomResultValid: Signature valid — CBOM is authentic and unmodified.
verifier.cbomResultInvalid: Signature invalid — CBOM may have been tampered with.

playground.metaDescription: Interactive browser playground for hybrid post-quantum encryption: X25519 + ML-KEM-1024 / McEliece / FrodoKEM + AES-256-GCM. With tamper mode, KEM race, 20× benchmark, and attacker view — fully client-side, no backend required.
playground.label: // hybrid encryption
playground.title: Hybrid Encryption Playground
playground.intro: An interactive playground that visualises hybrid post-quantum encryption step by step. Fully client-side — all computation runs in the browser, no backend required. Choose a KEM algorithm (ML-KEM-1024, Classic McEliece, or FrodoKEM), enter a message, and click "Generate". The playground combines X25519 (ECDH) with a PQC KEM — both shared secrets are combined via HKDF-SHA-256 into a single AES-256-GCM key. You will see exactly what is transmitted publicly and what remains private. Then: tamper mode (change one byte — the protocol breaks immediately), attacker and quantum-computer view, KEM race (all three in parallel), or a 20× benchmark with full statistics.
playground.mcElieceWarning: Warning: Classic McEliece key generation is computationally intensive — expect 10–30 seconds.
playground.mcElieceGeneratingNote: Key generation takes 10–30 seconds depending on your device. This is expected — McEliece keygen is inherently expensive.
playground.step1Desc: Both parties independently generate their key pairs. Private keys never leave their owner.
playground.step2Desc: Elliptic-curve cryptography. The client and server each multiply their private key by the other party's public key — the result is identical. Warning: a quantum computer running Shor's algorithm can break this step.
playground.step3Desc: Lattice-based algorithm (NIST FIPS 203, formerly Kyber-1024). No Diffie-Hellman — the client encapsulates a random secret into the server's public key. Only the server can extract it using its private key. Security level 5 (≥ AES-256) — the strongest ML-KEM variant.
playground.step3DescMcEliece: Code-based algorithm using binary Goppa codes, first proposed by Robert McEliece in 1978 — the oldest post-quantum candidate. No lattices, no Diffie-Hellman. Its main drawback is the enormous public key (~1.3 MB); the ciphertext is tiny by comparison (240 bytes). Quantum computers cannot efficiently break the underlying decoding hardness assumption.
playground.step3DescFrodoKem: Lattice-based algorithm (NIST Round 3 finalist, not standardised). No Diffie-Hellman — the client encapsulates a random secret into the server's public key; only the server can decapsulate it. Unlike ML-KEM, FrodoKEM uses unstructured plain LWE instead of Module-LWE: no algebraic structure, so no algebraic attack vectors. FrodoKEM-1344-AES targets security level 5 (≥ AES-256) — comparable to Classic McEliece 8192128. The trade-off: very large keys (~21 KB public key).
playground.step4Desc: Both shared secrets are combined into a single 256-bit AES key using HKDF-SHA256. Security equals the maximum of the two: an attacker would need to break both X25519 and the chosen KEM.
playground.step5Desc: The combined key encrypts the message using AES-256-GCM (Authenticated Encryption). A random 12-byte IV ensures that identical messages produce different ciphertexts.
playground.step6Desc: The recipient performs X25519 DH and KEM decapsulation, derives the same combinedKey, and decrypts — identical plaintext.
playground.howToStep1: Client and server independently generate their key pairs: an X25519 key pair for classical Diffie-Hellman and a KEM key pair (ML-KEM, McEliece, or FrodoKEM) for post-quantum security. Private keys never leave their owner.
playground.howToStep2: Client and server exchange their public X25519 keys and independently compute the same X25519 shared secret. This step is not quantum-safe — Shor's algorithm can break X25519 on a cryptographically relevant quantum computer.
playground.howToStep3: The client encapsulates a random shared secret into the server's public KEM key (encapsulation). The server decapsulates using its private KEM key (decapsulation). Both parties now share the same KEM shared secret without ever transmitting it.
playground.howToStep4: The X25519 shared secret and KEM shared secret are combined into a single 256-bit AES key using HKDF-SHA256. Security equals the stronger of the two: an attacker would need to break both X25519 and the KEM.
playground.howToStep5: The combined key encrypts the plaintext message using AES-256-GCM — an authenticated encryption scheme. A random 12-byte initialization vector (IV) ensures identical messages produce different ciphertexts.
playground.howToStep6: The recipient computes X25519 DH with their private key, decapsulates the KEM ciphertext, derives the same combinedKey via HKDF, and decrypts the AES-GCM ciphertext — yielding the original plaintext.
playground.implArticleIntro: How this algorithm works internally is covered in
playground.implArticleComingSoon: // A deep-dive article on Classic McEliece is coming soon.
playground.implArticleComingSoonFrodoKem: // A deep-dive article on FrodoKEM is coming soon.
playground.mlkemImplProse: Pure TypeScript, no WASM — the algorithm runs natively without any binary. The library is audited and follows the NIST FIPS 203 specification exactly. In this playground the computation runs in a Web Worker to keep the main thread responsive. One small quirk: the import path requires the .js suffix even in TypeScript projects — this is an ESM convention of the entire @noble family.
playground.mcElieceImplProse: This package wraps the official Classic McEliece C reference implementation compiled to WebAssembly via Emscripten — hence the ~3 MB WASM bundle and long keygen times. The entire API is async because WASM module initialisation is asynchronous. In this playground the computation runs in a Web Worker so the browser tab stays fully responsive even while McEliece blocks the worker thread for up to 30 seconds.
playground.frodoKemImplProse: @oqs/liboqs-js is a WebAssembly wrapper around the liboqs C library from the Open Quantum Safe (OQS) project — the most comprehensive PQC reference implementation available. The WASM is inlined as Base64 (no external .wasm file), which keeps bundling simple. The API follows an async factory pattern: createFrodoKEM1344AES() loads and initialises the WASM module; generateKeyPair() and encapsulate() are then synchronous. Important: kem.destroy() explicitly frees WASM heap memory — without it you get a memory leak.
playground.step4QuantumNote: Even though X25519 is broken — the KEM share remains secret. The combined key holds.
playground.mallorySummaryNeeds: To read the plaintext, she would need to:
playground.malloryCantBreakMlkem: ML-KEM: Solve the LWE problem (no known efficient algorithm, not even for quantum computers)
playground.malloryCantBreakMceliece: Classic McEliece: Decode a binary Goppa code (unbroken since 1978, robust against quantum attacks)
playground.malloryCantBreakFrodokem: FrodoKEM: Solve the plain-LWE problem (more conservative than Module-LWE, no known quantum attack)
playground.mallorySummaryQuantum: ⚡ With a quantum computer: X25519 is broken — but the KEM share remains secure. The combined HKDF key is only as weak as its strongest factor.
playground.tamperHint: Click any public hex field, change one or more bytes — and watch how the manipulation cascades through the protocol.
playground.tamperNoState: ✗ No active run. Please generate first.
playground.tamperStep3WrongNote: ⚠ KEM ciphertext tampered → server decapsulates wrong secret
playground.tamperStep4WrongNote: ⚠ Wrong combined key — derived from incorrect KEM secret
playground.tamperStep6AesFail: AES-GCM Authentication Tag Mismatch — the server detects the manipulation immediately. A single changed byte is enough to invalidate the entire ciphertext.
playground.tamperStep6KemFail: AES-GCM Authentication Tag Mismatch — the server derived a different combined key (wrong KEM secret → wrong HKDF input), so the GCM authentication tag does not match.
playground.realWorldIntro: Hybrid post-quantum encryption is not a future project — it is already active in billions of connections. The entries below are a selection of well-known examples, not a complete list.
playground.realWorldChromeDesc: X25519 + ML-KEM-768 in TLS 1.3 — enabled by default since April 2024. You are using it right now.
playground.realWorldSignalDesc: X25519 + Kyber-1024 via PQXDH — active for all messages since September 2023.
playground.realWorldAppleDesc: X25519 + ML-KEM-768 via PQ3 protocol — since iOS 17.4 / macOS 14.4 (February 2024).
playground.realWorldNistDesc: ML-KEM officially standardised in August 2024. Successor to Kyber. Basis of all modern implementations.
playground.realWorldMetaDesc: ML-KEM-768 in encrypted backups and calls — rollout from 2024.
playground.tooltipMean: The average of all 20 measurements. Sensitive to outliers — a single unusually high value pulls the mean upward, even if the remaining 19 measurements are tightly clustered.
playground.tooltipMedian: The middle value when all 20 measurements are sorted by size. More robust than the mean: outliers have little effect on the median, which often better reflects typical runtime behaviour.
playground.tooltipStd: The standard deviation measures how far measurements deviate from the mean on average. The n−1 (rather than n) denominator corrects the statistical estimation bias in small samples — here 20 measurements.
playground.tooltipCV: The standard deviation divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage. A CV below 10% indicates stable, reproducible measurements — values above 30% suggest high system-induced variance, such as from browser scheduling or WASM initialisation.
playground.tooltipCI95: The range in which the true mean lies with 95% probability. Calculated using the t-distribution (df=19, t=2.093) — more precise than the normal distribution for small samples. A narrow CI means the measurements are consistent.
playground.tooltipMinMax: The smallest and largest measured values from the 20 runs. Together with the IQR, they show how wide the overall spectrum of measured runtimes is.
playground.tooltipIQR: The interquartile range covers the middle 50% of all measurements — from Quartile 1 (25th percentile) to Quartile 3 (75th percentile). A small IQR means most measurements are tightly clustered, regardless of individual outliers.
playground.tooltipOutliers: Measurements that lie more than 1.5 times the IQR below Q1 or above Q3. These values are statistically unusual and may indicate browser interference, garbage collection, or WASM loading effects.
playground.tooltipTrimmed: The mean after removing outliers. Gives a more realistic picture of typical runtime than the raw mean — especially relevant for algorithms with high CV like Classic McEliece.
playground.benchmarkNote: Sequential runs (ML-KEM → FrodoKEM → McEliece) · 95%-CI: t-distribution df=19, t=2.093 · McEliece may take several minutes
playground.sigXmssLmsNote: // XMSS and LMS are not implemented: no actively maintained JS package exists for XMSS (last update 2018), and no browser-compatible one for LMS. SLH-DSA covers the same hash-based use case — NIST-standardized and actively supported.
playground.sigIntro: An interactive playground for digital post-quantum signatures. Fully client-side. Choose an algorithm, enter a message, and click 'Sign'. You will see key generation, the signing process, and verification step by step — including tamper mode (one change is enough for verification to fail immediately) and attacker and quantum-computer views.
playground.sigStep1Desc: The signer generates a key pair (secret key + public key). The public key is sent to the verifier — the secret key never leaves the signer.
playground.sigStep2DescEcdsa: The message is hashed externally with SHA-256 before ECDSA signs the hash with the private key using elliptic-curve arithmetic based on the discrete logarithm problem.
playground.sigStep2DescMlDsa65: ML-DSA hashes the message internally with SHAKE-256 and generates the signature over the module lattice problem (Module-LWE), where rejection sampling ensures that no information about the private key leaks.
playground.sigStep2DescSlhDsa: SLH-DSA hashes internally with SHA-256 and constructs the signature as a cryptographic proof over a multi-level hypertree of one-time signatures (WOTS+) and a few-time signature scheme (FORS) — without algebraic structures, relying exclusively on hash functions.
playground.sigStep3DescEcdsa: The verifier independently recomputes SHA-256(message) and then calls p256.verify(sig, SHA-256(msg), pk). Only if message, signature, and public key all match is the result valid.
playground.sigStep3Desc: The verifier passes the signature, message, and public key to verify(). The library hashes the message internally and checks whether the signature matches the public key. A valid signature proves: the message came from the signer and has not been altered.
playground.sigResultValid: ✓  Signature valid — content is authentic and unaltered
playground.sigResultInvalid: ✗  Signature invalid — content was altered or signature is forged
playground.sigTamperHint: Click the message or signature and modify it — verification fails immediately.
playground.sigStep1QuantumBadgeEcdsa:
⚡ Shor can compute
SK from PK
playground.sigStep3QuantumWarningEcdsa: ⚡ With a quantum computer: ECDSA is broken. Shor's algorithm can derive the secret key from the public key — signatures can no longer be trusted.
playground.ecdsaImplProse: Pure JavaScript, no WASM. @noble/curves is an audited, minimal cryptography library with no external dependencies. The import path @noble/curves/nist.js contains P-256, P-384, and P-521. Important: p256.sign() expects a pre-computed hash by default — with '{prehash: true}' the message is hashed internally with SHA-256 before ECDSA is applied.
playground.mldsa65ImplProse: Pure TypeScript, no WASM. ML-DSA-65 is NIST FIPS 204, based on the CRYSTALS-Dilithium algorithm. The library hashes the message internally with SHAKE-256 (per FIPS 204 §5.2) — no manual hashing required. The import path requires the .js suffix, even in TypeScript projects.
playground.slhdsa128sImplProse: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s (NIST FIPS 205) is a hash-based signature — no lattices, no elliptic curves. Security relies solely on SHA-2. The suffix '-128s' stands for 'small': tiny keys (32/64 B), but very slow signing (~85 ms). Compared to SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f: same keys, 10× smaller signature (7856 vs. 17088 B), but 10× slower. Dynamically imported from @noble/post-quantum.
playground.slhdsa128fImplProse: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f (NIST FIPS 205) is a hash-based signature. The suffix '-128f' stands for 'fast': faster signing (~8 ms) at the cost of a much larger signature (17088 B). Compared to SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s: same keys, 10× larger signature, but 10× faster. The speed/size trade-off within the same algorithm is pedagogically particularly interesting. Dynamically imported from @noble/post-quantum.
playground.sigImplArticleComingSoonEcdsa: // A deep-dive article on ECDSA P-256 is coming soon.
playground.sigImplArticleComingSoonMlDsa65: // How ML-DSA-65 works internally is covered in the ML-DSA article (coming soon).
playground.sigImplArticleComingSoonSlhDsa128s: // A deep-dive article on SLH-DSA is coming soon.
playground.sigImplArticleComingSoonSlhDsa128f: // A deep-dive article on SLH-DSA is coming soon.
playground.sigBenchmarkNote: Measured sequentially (ECDSA → ML-DSA → SLH-DSA-128f → SLH-DSA-128s) · 95% CI: t-distribution, df=19, t=2.093 · SLH-DSA-128s may take several seconds
playground.sigRealWorldIntro: Digital signatures are already everywhere — every HTTPS connection, every software update, every signed document relies on them. Post-quantum standards are actively being integrated.
playground.sigRealWorldTlsDesc: Every browser verifies the server's certificate signature on every HTTPS connection. NIST FIPS 204 (ML-DSA) is the designated post-quantum replacement for ECDSA in TLS.
playground.sigRealWorldCodeSignDesc: Windows Authenticode, macOS Gatekeeper, and Linux package managers (apt, rpm) all verify ECDSA/RSA signatures before installing software. PQC migration is underway across the industry.
playground.sigRealWorldEidasDesc: EU regulation eIDAS mandates legally binding digital signatures for contracts and official documents. ENISA recommends ML-DSA as the post-quantum migration path.
playground.sigRealWorldDnssecDesc: Every DNS zone can be signed to prevent cache poisoning attacks. ICANN is actively researching how to migrate DNSSEC to post-quantum algorithms.
playground.sigRealWorldSshDesc: OpenSSH uses ECDSA and Ed25519 for server authentication and user identity. OpenSSH 9.9 (2024) introduced experimental support for ML-DSA-65 host and user keys.
playground.step3EncapNote: // internally: fresh random bytes, ephemeral — unique to this session
playground.step3EncapSent: // ciphertext → transmitted to server (visible on the wire)
playground.step3DecapEphNote: sk_server is ephemeral — discarded after this step
playground.step3PfsText: Both sides use ephemeral keys — freshly generated per session and immediately discarded. An adversary who records the full network traffic of this session and later compromises all keys can still not decrypt past sessions: the ephemeral keys no longer exist.
playground.step3PfsX25519: X25519: client generates an ephemeral key pair → private key deleted immediately after the DH step
playground.step3PfsKem: KEM: server generates an ephemeral KEM key pair → private key deleted immediately after decapsulation
playground.step3PfsCombined: Independent mechanisms: an attacker must break both at runtime — retroactive compromise is impossible
playground.step3PfsMcElieceIntro: Classic McEliece key generation takes 10–30 s — per-session ephemeral KEM keys are not practical in production. The server key pair is typically static.
playground.step3PfsMcElieceKemWarn: KEM (McEliece) static: an adversary who stores the traffic and later obtains the private KEM key can reconstruct kemSS — no KEM-side PFS.
playground.step3PfsMcElieceX25519: X25519: client-side ephemeral key pair → private key irretrievably gone after the DH step → X25519 provides PFS unconditionally.
playground.step3PfsMcElieceCombined: Hybrid remains PFS-secure: HKDF requires x25519SS AND kemSS. Knowing only kemSS (static key compromised) is not enough to compute the session key — x25519SS no longer exists.
playground.step3PfsMcElieceDemoNote: In this demo, McEliece keys are regenerated each session — this is not representative of production deployments.

cbom.metaDescription: CBOM of beyond-shor.eu — {count} cryptographic assets documented per CycloneDX 1.6. Quantum-safety status of every component at a glance.
cbom.intro: This page documents the {count} cryptographic components actively used on beyond-shor.eu — from the post-quantum signature algorithms working in the background to the classical building blocks in the playground. Taking crypto-agility seriously means knowing what you have. This is our answer: a machine-readable cryptography inventory you can download, inspect, and analyse with CBOMkit-coeus.
cbom.idSchemeNote: Each component carries a context-scoped ID following the pattern algorithm:usage — for example aes-256-gcm:playground. This ensures the same cryptographic primitive can appear as a distinct entry in different deployment contexts without risking ID collisions.
cbom.scopeNote: The number {count} is a precision, not a limitation: not every library ends up in the CBOM — only the algorithms that perform actual cryptographic work on beyond-shor.eu. The TLS layer of the hosting provider, internal Node.js hashes for package integrity, and build tooling are deliberately out of scope.
cbom.autoUpdateNote: The inventory is updated daily by an automated scanner that analyses the entire codebase for cryptographic primitives. New algorithms appear automatically, removed ones disappear — no manual effort required.

rbe.metaDescription: Interactive demo for understanding Registration-Based Encryption (RBE): a post-quantum secure encryption paradigm without key escrow.
rbe.intro: This demo shows how Registration-Based Encryption (RBE) works — a lattice-based cryptosystem that models the Key Curator as a transparent, non-trusted party. All user operations run locally in the browser; KC operations run server-side — exactly as in a real deployment.
rbe.demoPlaceholderText: The step-by-step demo (Setup → Registration → Encryption → Helper Key → Decryption) will be implemented here.
rbe.paramsSubtitle: This demo uses reduced parameters for browser compatibility. The table below shows what a production system would look like according to the underlying research paper.
rbe.param_q_explain: Determines the noise budget for homomorphic operations. The paper requires nearly 60 bits so that accumulated errors never exceed the decryption threshold.
rbe.param_nR_explain: Degree of the cyclotomic polynomial. n_R = 1024 matches the standard for modern lattice-based cryptography (Kyber, Dilithium) and provides a high quantum-security level.
rbe.param_d_explain: Decomposition base of the gadget matrix G. d = 64 instead of d = 2 is the key optimisation in the paper: more compact ciphertexts and faster decryption — at the cost of faster noise growth.
rbe.param_n_explain: Module rank of the underlying Ring-LWE problem. Higher n increases security against structural attacks.
rbe.param_m_explain: Number of LWE samples (approx. n · log q). More samples provide more stable error tolerance as the number of users grows.
rbe.param_l_explain: Maximum length of a user identity in bits/characters. l = 50 covers most enterprise use cases (IDs, short email addresses).
rbe.param_N_explain: RBE is a bounded-N system: N_max is fixed at setup and determines the mpk size. For 1,000 users the paper reports a ciphertext size of ~0.148 MB.
rbe.securityNote: Demo parameters are mathematically correct but not cryptographically secure. Do not use in production.

rbePlayground.busyAttack1: Alice applying hsk_alice to c_bob (wrong identity binding!)…
rbePlayground.introComment: // what is rbe — and why no certificate?
rbePlayground.step0LabelA0: a0 — public reference point (Common Reference String)
rbePlayground.step0CalloutNmaxPost: users can be registered (the bounded-N property of RBE).
rbePlayground.step13CalloutSuffix: All three users submitted only their public key — the KC never saw a single secret key. A classical PKI approach would have required three CA signatures, three certificates, and three CRL entries. Here: a single
rbePlayground.step4Title: Encryption: Charlie writes to Bob and Alice
rbePlayground.step4CommentR: // r is chosen fresh for each message:
rbePlayground.step5CommentGt: // g_t = identity polynomial of the recipient:
rbePlayground.step5CommentGtInv: // g_t⁻¹ is the multiplicative inverse of g_t in R_q:
rbePlayground.step5CommentResult: // This yields the key property (easy to verify):
rbePlayground.step6Title: Security proof: Alice tries to read Bob's message
rbePlayground.step6AttackHskNote: was already loaded from the KC in step 5. Alice is now applying it to the wrong ciphertext.
rbePlayground.step6SuccessEmpty: Alice receives empty output — the noise completely overwhelms the message.
rbePlayground.btnAttack1: Step 1: apply hsk_alice to c_bob (identity mismatch!)
rbePlayground.decStep1OnlyHsk: The message cannot yet be read with hsk alone — sk is still missing.
